description: Both hands close around a wet cloth, and water leaves it. 'peki' brings pressure inward around an object or from opposing sides. 'pesa' (push) sends pressure mainly along one direction, so pressing a door and squeezing a sponge are different actions.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release them into stressed middle-height front 'e'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k', release it, and finish with high-front unrounded 'i'.
sound_symbolism: Two unvoiced stops frame the word, first at the lips and then at the soft palate. Their quick closures make the two syllables feel compact, like pressure applied twice.
usage_notes: What is compressed stands before the verb. A hand or press can appear with 'roe'. Pinching narrows the squeeze to a small area; crushing adds damage or breakage. As an event noun, 'peki' is a squeeze.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Hand pressure and simple presses extract juice or oil by the same compression. 'peki' can name the body's work or the tool's.

semantic_domains.activity: compressing a person or thing from opposing directions
semantic_domains.physical: pressure brought inward around a body or object
